back_translation: When it was decided among them that we would sail by ship to the country of Italy, they handed over Paul and some other prisoners to a centurion named Julius, of the Imperial Regiment.

We then boarded a ship from the city of Adramyttium, which was about to sail to various port cities along the coast of the province of Asia, and we set sail. Aristarchus, a Macedonian from the city of Thessalonica, traveled together with us.
The next day, we landed at the city of Sidon. Julius treated Paul with kindness, allowing him to go to his friends and receive care from them.
When we set out from there, we sailed along the northern coast of the island of Cyprus, because the wind was blowing against us.
When we had sailed across the open sea along the coastline of the province of Cilicia and the province of Pamphylia, we arrived at the city of Myra in the province of Lycia.
There the centurion found a ship from the city of Alexandria, which was about to sail to the country of Italy, so he had us transfer onto that ship.

We sailed slowly for many days and reached the city of Cnidus with difficulty, because the wind would not allow us to sail further. So we sailed along the southern coast of the island of Crete, opposite the cape of Salmone.
We sailed along the coast of that island with difficulty, until we came to a place called «Fair Harbors,» near the city of Lasea.
A considerable amount of time had passed. By that time the voyage had become risky and dangerous, because the day of fasting had also already passed. Therefore Paul warned them,
saying to them, «Sirs, I see that this voyage will bring damage and great loss — not only of the cargo and the ship, but also of our lives.»
But the centurion believed the captain and the owner of the ship more than the words that Paul had spoken.

Since that harbor was not suitable for spending the winter, the majority decided to set sail from there, hoping that, if possible, they could sail to the port city of Phoenix in order to spend the winter there. Phoenix was a harbor on the island of Crete, opening out toward the southwest and the northwest.
When a gentle south wind began to blow, they thought that the plan they had set was now possible, so they pulled up the anchor and sailed closely along the island of Crete.
But not long afterward, a violent cyclonic wind blew down from the island. This wind is called «the Eurakylon Wind.»
When the storm had carried the ship away and it was unable to resist the force of the wind, we let the wind drive us along.
When we sailed under the lee of a small island called Cauda, we were just barely able to secure the lifeboat with great difficulty.

When they had hoisted the lifeboat onto the main ship, they used ropes to bind around the hull of the ship. They were afraid of being driven by the wind into the quicksand-shoals of Syrtis, so they lowered the sea-anchor and let the wind drive them along in that manner.
As the storm continued to batter us violently, the next day they began to throw the cargo into the sea.
On the third day, they also threw the equipment of the ship into the sea with their own hands.
When neither the sun nor the stars shone visibly for many days, and the great storm continued to rage without stopping, in the end the hope of our surviving ran out completely.

When they had been without food for a long time, Paul stood up in the midst of them and said, «Sirs, you should have obeyed me and not set sail from the island of Crete; then you would have avoided this great damage and loss.»
Now I urge you all to take courage, for there will be no one among you who must lose his life — only the ship will be lost.
For this very night, an angel of the God whose servant I am and whom I serve, came and stood beside me.
The angel said, ‹Do not be afraid, Paul. You must go and stand before Caesar; and behold, God has already granted you the lives of all those who are sailing with you.›
Therefore, sirs, take courage. I believe God, that it will be just as he spoke to me.
But we will have to run aground on a certain island.»
When the fourteenth night came, while we were still being driven along in the Adria Sea, around midnight, the sailors began to sense that they were drawing near to land.
So they sounded the depth of the water and found that it was twenty fathoms deep. After sailing on a little, they sounded again, and found it was fifteen fathoms deep.
They were afraid that the ship would strike against rocks, so they cast four anchors from the stern, and prayed that daybreak would come quickly.
At that time, the sailors tried to flee from the ship. They lowered the lifeboat down into the sea, pretending that they were going to cast anchors from the bow.
Paul therefore said to the centurion and to the soldiers, «If these men do not stay on the ship, you will not be able to survive.»
So the soldiers cut the ropes that tied the lifeboat, and let it float away loose.
Until daybreak was near, Paul kept urging everyone to eat food, saying, «Today is the fourteenth day in which you have been waiting in anxiety, without having eaten anything at all.
Therefore I urge you to eat some food, because doing this will help you survive; for not even a single hair from the head of any of you will fall.»
When Paul had said this, he took up bread, gave thanks to God in front of everyone, then broke it and began to eat.
Everyone took courage, and they all began to eat together as well.
On that ship, we were altogether two hundred and seventy-six persons.
When they had eaten their fill of food, they made the ship lighter by throwing the wheat into the sea.
When daybreak came, the sailors did not recognize the land. But they saw a certain bay that had a sandy beach. They decided together that, if possible, they would bring the ship aground there.

So they pulled up the anchors and left them in the sea, while loosening the ropes that tied the rudders, and they hoisted the bow-sail to catch the wind that was blowing, heading straight toward the sand-beach.
But the ship struck a sandbar that was between two water-currents, and ran aground there. The bow was firmly stuck in place, while the stern was being broken to pieces by the waves.
The soldiers planned to kill the prisoners, fearing that some might swim away and escape.
But the centurion wanted to preserve Paul's life, so he prevented them from carrying out that plan, and ordered those who could swim to jump into the water first, in order to reach the shore.
As for the rest, he had them follow — some clinging to wooden planks, others clinging to various parts of the ship. In this way, all of them came safely to the land.
